column1: fact
Cleopatra lived closer in time to the Moon landing than to the building of the Great Pyramid.
Oxford University is older than the Aztec Empire.
Ancient Egyptians used moldy bread as antibiotic medicine, unknowingly harnessing penicillin thousands of years before its official discovery.
The Great Wall of China is not visible from space with the naked eye, despite popular belief.
Roman concrete gets stronger over time due to a special volcanic ash ingredient that continues to react with seawater.
Ancient Greeks invented the world's first computer - a bronze device called the Antikythera mechanism that could predict eclipses.
Maya mathematicians invented the concept of zero independently, centuries before it appeared in Europe.
Ancient Chinese invented toilet paper, but it was reserved exclusively for emperors.
The Library of Alexandria had a law requiring all ships entering the harbor to surrender their books for copying.
Aztec warriors could achieve higher social status through capturing enemies alive rather than killing them.
Ancient Mesopotamians invented the 60-minute hour and 360-degree circle, which we still use today.
Indian mathematicians calculated pi to four decimal places over 1,000 years before Europeans.
Ancient Egyptians kept lions, cheetahs, and baboons as household pets.
Roman soldiers were sometimes paid in salt, which is where the word 'salary' comes from.
The ancient Greek Olympics lasted for nearly 1,200 years without interruption.
Maya cities had sophisticated water management systems with pressurized fountains.
Ancient Chinese silk was so valuable that revealing silk-making secrets was punishable by death.
The ancient Indian game of chess was originally played with dice.
Mesopotamian beer was so thick it had to be drunk through straws to filter out chunks.
Ancient Egyptians invented breath mints made from frankincense, myrrh, and cinnamon.
Roman women wore bikinis for athletic competitions, as shown in 4th-century mosaics.
Ancient Greeks used to vote people into exile just for being too popular or powerful.
The Aztec capital Tenochtitlan was larger than any European city of its time.
Ancient Chinese invented ice cream by mixing rice, milk, and snow.
Maya blue paint was so chemically stable that it still retains its vivid color after 1,000 years.
Ancient Indians performed cataract surgery using a curved needle technique.
Mesopotamians created the first known recipe for beer over 4,000 years ago.
Ancient Egyptians invented the 365-day calendar that forms the basis of our modern calendar.
Roman concrete was waterproof and could set underwater, enabling massive harbor construction.
Ancient Greeks believed redheads turned into vampires after death.
The Maya had a more accurate calendar than the one we use today.
Ancient Chinese invented gunpowder but primarily used it for fireworks and scaring away evil spirits.
Indian surgeons performed plastic surgery and documented techniques for nose reconstruction.
Ancient Mesopotamians invented the wheel, but used it for pottery for 300 years before transportation.
Egyptian pharaohs were buried with golden sandals because they believed they would walk on golden floors in the afterlife.
Roman gladiators were mostly vegetarians who drank a calcium-rich ash drink for bone strength.
Ancient Greeks invented the alarm clock using water that dropped pebbles onto a gong.
Aztec children attended mandatory public schools, making them more literate than most Europeans.
Ancient Chinese invented paper money because carrying thousands of bronze coins was impractical.
Maya astronomers predicted solar eclipses with greater accuracy than European astronomers of the same period.
Ancient Indians invented shampoo using a mixture of herbs and natural ingredients.
Mesopotamian doctors performed brain surgery and documented their procedures on clay tablets.
Ancient Egyptians used pregnancy tests made from wheat and barley seeds that were 70% accurate.
Roman roads were built so well that many are still in use today, 2,000 years later.
Ancient Greeks invented the vending machine to dispense holy water in temples.
The Aztec Empire collected taxes in the form of cocoa beans, feathers, and precious stones.
Ancient Chinese invented the compass but initially used it for feng shui, not navigation.
Maya mathematicians used a base-20 number system instead of our base-10 system.
Ancient Indians invented the decimal system and place-value notation that we use today.
Mesopotamians created the first known maps of the world on clay tablets.
Ancient Egyptians invented toothpaste made from crushed eggshells and animal hooves.
Roman engineers built heated floors and walls using a system called hypocaust.
Ancient Greeks invented the odometer to measure distances traveled by chariots.
Aztec merchants had their own courts and laws separate from the regular legal system.
Ancient Chinese invented earthquake detectors that could sense tremors from hundreds of miles away.
Medieval Islamic scholars invented algebra, and the word itself comes from the Arabic 'al-jabr' meaning 'reunion of broken parts.'
Vikings reached North America 500 years before Columbus, establishing settlements in what is now Newfoundland.
The University of Oxford is older than the Aztec Empire by over 200 years.
During the Tang Dynasty, China's capital Chang'an was the world's largest city with over one million residents.
Medieval Islamic hospitals had the world's first medical schools and required doctors to pass exams to practice medicine.
Mongol messengers could travel from one end of their empire to the other - roughly the distance from Portugal to Korea - using an organized relay system.
Viking women could request divorces and own property, giving them more legal rights than many European women would have for centuries.
The House of Wisdom in Baghdad had the world's largest library during the 9th century, preserving Greek texts that would have otherwise been lost.
Medieval European knights were often shorter than modern 12-year-olds due to poor nutrition.
The Mongols were religiously tolerant and exempted religious leaders of all faiths from paying taxes.
Tang Dynasty China invented gunpowder, but initially used it primarily for fireworks and religious ceremonies.
Medieval Islamic cities had street lighting, public hospitals, and running water while most European cities had none of these.
Vikings used a crystal called Iceland spar to navigate in cloudy weather, acting as a natural polarizing filter to locate the sun.
The Mongol Empire was the largest contiguous land empire in history, covering 16% of the Earth's total land area.
Medieval European monasteries preserved brewing techniques that created many beer styles still enjoyed today.
Islamic Golden Age scholars calculated the Earth's circumference to within 200 miles of the actual measurement.
Vikings discovered that heating their swords in peat fires created a form of carbon steel superior to most medieval weapons.
The Tang Dynasty established the world's first government-issued paper money.
Medieval Islamic mathematician Al-Khwarizmi's name gave us the word 'algorithm.'
Mongol women could become shamans, merchants, and even military leaders, with some ruling as regents.
Medieval European cathedrals took so long to build that architectural styles would change completely during construction.
The Islamic Golden Age produced the first accurate maps of the world, correcting errors that had persisted since ancient times.
Vikings practiced a form of democracy where free men could vote at assemblies called 'things.'
The Mongols created the first international postal system, with relay stations spanning from Eastern Europe to the Pacific.
Tang Dynasty China had a female emperor, Wu Zetian, who ruled for 15 years and expanded the empire significantly.
Medieval Islamic scholars performed cataract surgery and wrote detailed medical textbooks used in Europe for centuries.
Vikings reached as far south as North Africa and as far east as Constantinople, trading amber for silk and spices.
The Mongols adopted and improved upon Chinese siege technology, making them nearly unstoppable in warfare.
Medieval European peasants had more vacation days than most modern workers due to numerous religious holidays.
Islamic Golden Age astronomers built observatories and created star charts more accurate than any produced in Europe until the Renaissance.
Vikings believed that dying in battle would earn them a place in Valhalla, but dying in bed meant going to the much less appealing realm of Hel.
The Mongol Empire facilitated trade along the Silk Road so effectively that a merchant could travel from Europe to China under the protection of a single authority.
Tang Dynasty China was so cosmopolitan that foreigners could take civil service exams and hold government positions.
Medieval Islamic cities had public libraries where anyone could borrow books, centuries before this concept appeared in Europe.
Vikings used to give their swords names and believed the weapons had souls.
The Mongols were among the first to use biological warfare, catapulting plague-infected corpses into besieged cities.
Medieval European kings often couldn't read or write, relying entirely on educated clergy for correspondence.
Islamic Golden Age scholars translated and preserved ancient Greek texts, later reintroducing them to Europe during the Renaissance.
Vikings navigated using a combination of ravens, whales, and wind patterns rather than relying solely on coastal landmarks.
The Mongol military was organized in decimal units, with groups of 10, 100, 1,000, and 10,000 warriors called tumens.
Tang Dynasty China invented woodblock printing, mass-producing books 400 years before Gutenberg.
Medieval Islamic merchants created the first forms of banking, including checks and credit systems.
Vikings considered poetry and storytelling as important as fighting skills, with skilled poets receiving great honor.
The Mongols promoted trade so successfully that Chinese porcelain and Islamic textiles became common in European markets.
Medieval European nobles often ate off of stale bread trenchers instead of plates, then gave the bread to servants or dogs.
Islamic Golden Age mathematicians developed trigonometry and advanced our understanding of optics and physics.
Vikings practiced elaborate grooming rituals and were considered quite vain by the standards of their time.
The Mongol Empire's communication system was so efficient that news could travel from one end to the other faster than in most modern countries before the telegraph.
Tang Dynasty China had a thriving international community with Korean, Japanese, Persian, and Arab residents in major cities.
Medieval Islamic scholars established the first universities with degrees, graduation ceremonies, and academic freedom.
Vikings used skis and ice skates for winter warfare, giving them mobility advantages in northern climates.
The Mongols were so successful at psychological warfare that many cities surrendered without fighting upon hearing of their approach.
Medieval European churches used gargoyles not just for decoration, but as functional water spouts to protect the building from rain damage.
Islamic Golden Age physicians performed complex surgeries and understood the circulation of blood centuries before European doctors.
Vikings established the world's first parliament in Iceland in 930 CE, called the Althing, which still exists today.
The Mongol Empire was so vast that it encompassed parts of 40 modern-day countries across four continents.
Christopher Columbus died believing he had reached Asia, never realizing he had encountered entirely new continents.
The Spanish Empire's silver from the Americas caused massive inflation in Europe and actually made Spain poorer in the long run.
Potatoes were considered poisonous by Europeans for 200 years because wealthy people got sick from lead poisoning when acidic potatoes leached lead from their pewter plates.
The Dutch East India Company was worth more than Apple, Google, and Microsoft combined in today's money, making it the most valuable company in history.
Tomatoes were called 'poison apples' by Europeans because aristocrats died after eating them - again due to lead poisoning from their plates, not the tomatoes themselves.
The Inca Empire had no written language, wheels, or iron tools, yet built roads and structures that surpassed European engineering of the time.
Chocolate was so valuable to the Aztecs that cacao beans were used as currency, and counterfeiting them was punishable by death.
The first African slaves in the Americas were actually brought by other Africans who served as intermediaries in the slave trade.
Magellan never completed the first circumnavigation of the globe - he died in the Philippines, and only 18 of his original 237 crew members made it back to Spain.
The word 'slave' comes from 'Slav' because so many Slavic people were enslaved by various empires that their ethnicity became synonymous with bondage.
Native Americans introduced Europeans to over 60 crops that now make up 75% of the world's food supply, including corn, potatoes, and tomatoes.
The Spanish conquistador Hernán Cortés burned his own ships to prevent his men from retreating, forcing them to conquer the Aztec Empire or die trying.
China's Ming Dynasty had massive treasure fleets with ships over 400 feet long decades before Columbus, but abandoned ocean exploration to focus inward.
The Portuguese were the first Europeans to reach Japan, and a single matchlock rifle they brought revolutionized Japanese warfare within decades.
Smallpox killed an estimated 90% of Native Americans, making it the most devastating pandemic in human history and largely emptying two continents for European settlement.
The spice nutmeg was once worth more than gold, and the Dutch traded Manhattan to the British for a tiny nutmeg-producing island called Run.
African kingdoms like Mali were so wealthy from gold trade that when Emperor Mansa Musa visited Cairo, he gave away so much gold he crashed the economy for a decade.
The first stock market crash in history was caused by tulip bulbs in the Netherlands, where a single bulb could cost more than a house.
Pirates had democratic governments with written constitutions, healthcare, and disability benefits centuries before most nations adopted such systems.
The Aztec capital Tenochtitlan was larger and cleaner than any European city of its time, with running water and waste management that amazed Spanish conquistadors.
Sugar was called 'white gold' and was so valuable that it drove more of the slave trade than any other commodity, including actual gold.
The first global currency was Spanish silver pieces of eight, which were used from China to the Caribbean and remained legal tender in the US until 1857.
Vasco da Gama's voyage to India was so profitable that it paid for itself 60 times over, sparking the European rush to find sea routes to Asia.
The Columbian Exchange introduced horses to the Americas, completely transforming Plains Indian culture within just a few generations.
European diseases reached the Americas faster than Europeans themselves, devastating indigenous populations before they even saw their first conquistador.
The Portuguese controlled the global black pepper trade so tightly that 'peppercorn rent' became a term for nominal payment, since pepper was incredibly valuable.
Captain Cook was killed and eaten by Native Hawaiians who initially worshipped him as a god but changed their minds when he returned unexpectedly.
The Spanish Empire was the first in history where 'the sun never set,' spanning from the Philippines to Peru across multiple time zones.
Tobacco was initially promoted by Europeans as a cure-all medicine that could treat everything from headaches to plague.
The Mayflower Compact was signed because the Pilgrims landed in the wrong place and were technically outside English law, forcing them to create their own government.
Indigenous Americans had been selectively breeding corn for thousands of years, transforming tiny grass seeds into the large kernels Europeans found.
The Treaty of Tordesillas divided the entire undiscovered world between Spain and Portugal based on a line drawn on a map, without asking anyone else.
Scurvy killed more sailors during the Age of Exploration than storms, battles, and all other diseases combined.
The Silk Road was actually a network of trade routes that connected Europe to Asia for over 1,400 years before Europeans found sea routes.
Vanilla comes from orchids that only grow naturally in Mexico, and the secret of pollinating them was known only to the Totonac people for centuries.
The Spanish Armada was defeated as much by storms as by English ships, leading to the phrase 'God blew and they were scattered.'
Coffee was introduced to Europe by Venetian merchants trading with Muslims, but was initially called 'Satan's drink' until the Pope blessed it.
The first European to see the Pacific Ocean was Vasco Núñez de Balboa, who claimed the entire ocean and all its shores for Spain.
Indigenous Australians had been living on the continent for 65,000 years before Europeans arrived, making them the world's oldest continuous civilization.
The East India Company had its own army that was twice the size of the British Army and controlled more territory than the British government.
Sailors believed that women on ships brought bad luck, yet many successful pirates were women, including Zheng Yi Sao who commanded over 80,000 pirates.
The first African American to set foot in what would become the United States was Estevanico, an explorer who guided Spanish expeditions through the Southwest.
European maps labeled unknown areas with 'Here be dragons' not because they believed in dragons, but because they feared the unknown dangers of uncharted waters.
The Inca Empire was connected by 25,000 miles of roads through mountains that were better engineered than Roman roads, all built without wheels or iron tools.
Blackbeard's real name was Edward Teach, and he deliberately cultivated his terrifying image by weaving hemp and lit matches into his beard during battle.
The first Thanksgiving feast included no turkey, potatoes, or cranberry sauce - it was mostly venison, fowl, corn, and shellfish.
European explorers often survived by eating their leather boots, belts, and even the ship's rats during long voyages when food ran out.
The Maroons were escaped slaves who established independent communities in the Americas and successfully fought off European colonial forces for centuries.
Indentured servants made up the majority of European immigrants to early America, working for years to pay off their passage before gaining freedom.
The Age of Exploration was largely funded by new banking techniques developed in Italian city-states, making exploration a capitalist venture from the start.
Many Native American tribes had democratic governments with women's suffrage and environmental protections centuries before European democracies adopted these concepts.
The first humans to cross the Pacific Ocean were Polynesian navigators who used stick charts to map ocean swells and reached islands 1,000 years before Magellan.
European demand for beaver hats nearly drove North American beavers to extinction and fundamentally altered the continent's waterways and ecosystems.
The Spanish brought the first cattle, pigs, and horses to the Americas, creating massive environmental changes as these animals spread across the continents.
Colonial Boston had a higher literacy rate than London because Puritan religious beliefs emphasized that everyone should be able to read the Bible.
The wealth extracted from the Americas allowed Europe to fund the Renaissance, making American gold and silver the foundation of European cultural achievements.
Anesthesia was so revolutionary that before its invention, the fastest surgeons were considered the best surgeons because speed meant survival.
Ancient Egyptians performed brain surgery and documented it in medical papyri over 4,000 years ago, making holes in skulls to relieve pressure.
The first successful heart transplant patient lived only 18 days, but the surgeon had practiced the procedure hundreds of times on dogs first.
Penicillin was discovered by accident when Alexander Fleming left a petri dish uncovered and mold contaminated his bacteria cultures.
Ancient Chinese doctors were paid only when their patients stayed healthy - if you got sick, you stopped paying your doctor.
The first vaccine was created by deliberately infecting people with cowpox to prevent the deadlier smallpox disease.
Doctors didn't start regularly washing their hands before surgery until the 1860s, and the doctor who suggested it was ridiculed by his peers.
The structure of DNA was discovered partly by using X-ray crystallography to photograph the molecule's shadow.
Ancient Romans performed cataract surgery by inserting a needle into the eye and pushing the clouded lens to the bottom of the eyeball.
The first successful use of insulin to treat diabetes happened in 1922, and within months it transformed the disease from fatal to manageable.
Bloodletting was considered proper medical treatment for over 2,000 years and was used to treat everything from headaches to mental illness.
The first recorded autopsy was performed in ancient Egypt, but it was done by embalmers, not doctors.
Aspirin was originally made from willow tree bark and was used by ancient Sumerians and Egyptians thousands of years ago.
The discovery of germs causing disease was so controversial that doctors rejected the idea for decades, preferring to blame 'bad air.'
Ancient Indian surgeons performed plastic surgery and nose reconstruction using techniques described in texts from 600 BCE.
The first successful vaccination campaign eliminated smallpox entirely from Earth - the only human disease ever completely eradicated by medicine.
Medieval Islamic physicians performed complex eye surgeries and wrote detailed medical textbooks that were used in Europe for centuries.
The invention of the microscope revealed an entire invisible world of microorganisms that no human had ever seen before.
Ancient Greek physicians believed that mental illness was caused by physical imbalances, not supernatural forces, making them remarkably modern in approach.
The first successful organ transplant was a kidney between identical twins, eliminating the problem of organ rejection.
Leeches are still used in modern medicine because their saliva contains natural blood thinners that help reattach severed body parts.
The discovery of blood circulation overturned 1,400 years of medical belief that blood was consumed by the body rather than circulated.
Ancient Mesopotamians created the first known medical prescriptions, writing them on clay tablets in cuneiform script.
The invention of the stethoscope happened because a French doctor was too embarrassed to put his ear directly on a woman's chest.
Digitalis, a crucial heart medication, was discovered by studying an old woman's herbal remedy for 'dropsy' in 18th-century England.
The first successful cesarean section where both mother and baby survived was performed by a pig gelder with no medical training.
Ancient Chinese physicians discovered that certain plants could prevent scurvy over 1,000 years before Europeans figured out the vitamin C connection.
The development of antiseptics reduced surgical death rates from 50% to less than 15% within just a few years.
Medieval Arab physicians invented the surgical needle, created the first hospitals with medical schools, and established the first pharmacy regulations.
The discovery of radioactivity led to both life-saving cancer treatments and the understanding that radiation could also cause cancer.
Ancient Egyptian physicians specialized in different body parts - there were separate doctors for eyes, teeth, and internal organs.
The first successful treatment for diabetes involved putting patients on starvation diets, which sometimes worked but often killed them faster.
Nitrous oxide was used as a party drug at 'laughing gas parties' for decades before anyone realized it could be used for painless surgery.
The discovery that ulcers are caused by bacteria, not stress or spicy food, was so controversial that the discoverer infected himself to prove it.
Ancient Roman military surgeons developed advanced trauma surgery techniques that weren't matched again until World War I.
The first recorded use of prosthetics dates to ancient Egypt, where archaeologists found a wooden toe attached to a mummy.
Quinine, the first effective malaria treatment, comes from the bark of the cinchona tree and was discovered by indigenous South Americans.
The invention of eyeglasses in medieval Italy was the first technology that actually improved human sensory capabilities beyond natural limits.
Ancient Chinese physicians used variolation - deliberately causing mild smallpox infections - to prevent severe cases centuries before Western vaccination.
The discovery of vitamins explained why sailors got scurvy and why certain populations thrived on seemingly inadequate diets.
Medieval European barber-surgeons performed surgery, pulled teeth, and cut hair because they were the only people skilled with sharp blades.
The development of anesthesia made it possible to perform longer, more complex surgeries, revolutionizing everything from childbirth to brain surgery.
Ancient Indian physicians described diabetes so accurately they named it 'madhumeha' - meaning honey urine - after noticing ants were attracted to diabetic patients' urine.
The discovery of blood types explained why some blood transfusions saved lives while others killed patients instantly.
Medieval Islamic hospitals were the first to separate patients by disease type and provide free medical care regardless of social status.
The invention of the iron lung kept polio patients alive by mechanically breathing for them when their chest muscles were paralyzed.
Ancient Greek physicians used opium for pain relief and described its effects so accurately that their observations guided medical use for centuries.
The discovery that childbed fever was caused by doctors not washing hands between autopsies and deliveries was rejected because it offended physicians' honor.
Medieval Chinese physicians developed inoculation by blowing powdered smallpox scabs into patients' noses through silver tubes.
The development of pasteurization not only made milk safe but proved that microorganisms cause food spoilage and disease.
Ancient Egyptian physicians performed successful cataract surgery using bronze needles and documented their techniques in medical papyri.
The discovery of chromosomes revealed that heredity was controlled by physical structures inside cells, not mysterious forces.
Medieval European physicians believed that mental illness could be cured by drilling holes in the skull to release evil spirits or excess pressure.
The invention of the thermometer finally gave doctors an objective way to measure fever instead of relying on touch.
Ancient Roman physicians used willow bark extract for pain relief, unknowingly using the same compound that would later become aspirin.
The discovery that scurvy could be prevented with citrus fruits saved more sailor lives than any naval battle victory in history.
The shortest war in history lasted only 38-45 minutes between Britain and Zanzibar in 1896.
More people died from the 1918 flu pandemic than in all of World War I.
Napoleon was actually average height for his time - the 'short' reputation came from differences between French and English measurements.
The Christmas Truce of 1914 saw enemy soldiers playing football together in No Man's Land during WWI.
Hannibal crossed the Alps with war elephants, but most died from the cold before reaching Rome.
The Mongol Empire killed so many people that forests regrew and global CO2 levels dropped measurably.
Vikings wore horned helmets only in operas - real Viking helmets were simple and practical.
The Hundred Years' War actually lasted 116 years.
More Confederate soldiers died from disease than from battle wounds during the American Civil War.
Spartan warriors were encouraged to have older male lovers to increase their fighting effectiveness.
Samurai warriors often fought with guns, not just swords - they adopted firearms enthusiastically.
The Trojan Horse was likely a metaphor for an earthquake, not an actual wooden horse.
Roman gladiators were mostly vegetarians and drank a sports drink made from plant ash.
The last cavalry charge in military history happened during World War II by Polish lancers against German tanks.
Camels were used in the American Civil War by the U.S. Army in the southwestern territories.
The crossbow was considered so inhumane that the Catholic Church tried to ban its use against Christians.
Japanese kamikaze pilots wore helmets despite flying suicide missions.
The Aztecs had mandatory education for all children, regardless of social class, while most of Europe did not.
Carrier pigeons were used to send messages during both World Wars and had a 95% success rate.
The ancient Greeks invented flamethrowers and used them in naval warfare.
More than half of all American casualties in the Vietnam War occurred after peace talks had already begun.
The Crusades introduced Europeans to soap, which Muslims had been using for centuries.
Genghis Khan killed so many people that he's considered history's most environmentally friendly conqueror.
The word 'assassin' comes from a medieval Islamic sect that used hashish before carrying out political murders.
Roman soldiers were paid in salt, which is where the word 'salary' comes from.
The longest siege in history lasted 21 years - the Ottoman siege of Candia in Crete.
Blackbeard the pirate only terrorized the seas for about two years before being killed.
The ancient Persians considered lying the most shameful act, even in warfare.
World War I soldiers suffered from 'shell shock,' now known as PTSD, but were often shot for cowardice instead of treated.
The Inca Empire had no written language, no wheels, and no iron tools, yet built a vast empire connected by roads.
Medieval knights rarely fought in armor - it was too expensive and most conflicts were quick raids.
The American Revolution was fought with weapons that took 30 seconds to reload after each shot.
Cleopatra spoke nine languages and was the first Ptolemaic ruler to actually learn Egyptian.
The Thirty Years' War reduced the population of the German states by 25-40%.
Ancient Chinese generals flew kites over enemy territory to measure distances for artillery.
The last person to die in the American Civil War was killed six months after Lee's surrender.
Berserkers likely achieved their battle fury through consuming psychoactive mushrooms, not just rage.
The Ottoman Empire had female admirals commanding naval fleets in the 16th century.
Ancient Greek fire was a secret weapon so effective that the formula was lost and never rediscovered.
The Siege of Leningrad lasted 872 days and people resorted to eating wallpaper paste to survive.
Mayan warriors captured enemies for sacrifice rather than killing them in battle whenever possible.
The longbow could penetrate armor at 200 yards and required a lifetime of training to master.
Battlefield medicine advanced more during the Civil War than in the previous 200 years combined.
The Charge of the Light Brigade was the result of a miscommunicated order during the Crimean War.
Ancient Roman military engineers could build a fortified camp for an entire legion in just a few hours.
The Zulu warriors at Rorke's Drift saluted the British defenders' bravery after their defeat.
Trebuchets could launch 300-pound stones over 300 yards and were more accurate than cannons.
The Celts fought naked in battle believing it gave them magical protection and intimidated enemies.
Sun Tzu's 'Art of War' was written 2,500 years ago but is still taught in modern military academies.
The Maginot Line worked perfectly - the Germans simply went around it through Belgium.
Ancient Assyrians invented siege warfare tactics still used today, including battering rams and siege towers.
The Pony Express lasted only 18 months before being replaced by the telegraph.
Medieval siege warfare sometimes lasted years, with attackers and defenders establishing permanent settlements.
The last duel fought with swords in France occurred in 1967 between two politicians over an insult.
Napoleon Bonaparte was actually average height for his time at 5'7", but appeared short because his guards were selected for being exceptionally tall.
Albert Einstein's brain was stolen by the pathologist who performed his autopsy and kept in jars for decades without family permission.
Cleopatra spoke at least nine languages fluently and was the first member of her dynasty to actually learn Egyptian.
Benjamin Franklin wanted the turkey, not the eagle, to be America's national bird because he thought eagles had bad moral character.
Mozart had a pet starling that could sing part of his Piano Concerto No. 17, and he held a funeral for it when it died.
Charles Darwin and Abraham Lincoln were born on the exact same day: February 12, 1809.
Vincent van Gogh only sold one painting during his lifetime, and it was to his brother's friend.
Marie Curie's research notebooks from the 1890s are still radioactive and will remain so for another 1,500 years.
Leonardo da Vinci wrote most of his notes in mirror script, readable only when held up to a mirror.
Winston Churchill was born in a ladies' cloakroom during a dance at Blenheim Palace because his mother went into labor unexpectedly.
Nikola Tesla was obsessed with the number three and would walk around buildings three times before entering them.
Ernest Hemingway survived two plane crashes in two days while on safari in Africa, and read his own obituaries in newspapers.
Theodore Roosevelt delivered a 90-minute speech immediately after being shot in the chest, saying 'It takes more than that to kill a Bull Moose.'
Frida Kahlo was the first 20th-century artist to have a work acquired by the Louvre, but she lied about her birth year to seem younger.
Mark Twain was born and died in years when Halley's Comet was visible, and he predicted this would happen.
Julius Caesar was once kidnapped by pirates, insulted them for setting his ransom too low, and later returned to crucify them.
Gandhi was nominated for the Nobel Peace Prize five times but never won; the committee later called this their greatest omission.
Edgar Allan Poe married his 13-year-old first cousin when he was 27 years old.
Michelangelo hated painting so much that he wrote a poem complaining about the physical pain of painting the Sistine Chapel ceiling.
Alexander Hamilton and Aaron Burr attended the same college (Columbia) and served together before their famous duel.
Jane Austen's identity as a novelist was kept secret during her lifetime; her books were published as 'By a Lady.'
Pablo Picasso's first word was 'piz' for pencil, and he could draw before he could walk properly.
Isaac Newton spent more time studying alchemy and biblical chronology than physics and mathematics.
Beethoven was completely deaf when he composed his 9th Symphony and had to be turned around to see the audience applauding.
Joan of Arc was about 17 years old when she convinced the French court to let her lead military campaigns.
Franklin D. Roosevelt concealed his paralysis so well that many Americans didn't know he used a wheelchair.
Coco Chanel lived in the Ritz Hotel in Paris for 37 years and died there at age 87.
Andy Warhol was shot and nearly killed by Valerie Solanas, but the incident was overshadowed by RFK's assassination the same day.
Catherine the Great of Russia was actually German and never learned to speak Russian without an accent.
Walt Disney was afraid of mice, despite creating Mickey Mouse as his most famous character.
Agatha Christie disappeared for 11 days in 1926, and when found, claimed to have amnesia about the entire incident.
Howard Hughes once bought an entire airline (TWA) just because he wanted to design better airplanes.
Josephine Baker smuggled intelligence for the French Resistance written in invisible ink on her sheet music during WWII.
Salvador Dalí was so afraid of grasshoppers that he would have panic attacks when he saw them.
Emily Dickinson wrote nearly 1,800 poems but published fewer than a dozen during her lifetime.
Steve Jobs was adopted and didn't meet his biological father until he was an adult, not knowing who he was.
Audrey Hepburn spoke five languages fluently and worked as a courier for the Dutch Resistance as a teenager.
Charlie Chaplin once entered a Charlie Chaplin look-alike contest and came in third place.
Eleanor Roosevelt was so shy as a child that she was afraid to go to school and was taught by private tutors.
Leonardo da Vinci was a vegetarian who would buy caged birds just to set them free.
Amelia Earhart worked as a nurse's aide, truck driver, and photographer before becoming a pilot.
Oscar Wilde was 6'3" tall, making him a giant for his era, and was an accomplished boxer at Oxford.
Helen Keller could identify her friends by their smell and communicated in multiple languages including French and German.
Ernest Shackleton advertised for his Antarctic expedition: 'Men wanted for hazardous journey. Low wages, bitter cold, long hours of complete darkness.'
Maya Angelou didn't speak for five years as a child after a traumatic experience, communicating only through writing.
Franklin Pierce was the only U.S. President to affirm rather than swear the oath of office due to his religious beliefs.
Josephine de Beauharnais had all her teeth removed and replaced with ivory ones, which gave her a lisp.
Edgar Degas was nearly blind in his later years but continued painting by feel and memory.
Mata Hari was likely innocent of espionage and was executed by France as a scapegoat during WWI.
Frederick Douglass didn't know his exact birth date and chose February 14th as his birthday.
Virginia Woolf wrote most of her novels while standing at a tall desk, believing it helped her think more clearly.
Rasputin survived being poisoned, shot, and beaten before finally drowning, according to his assassins' accounts.
Zora Neale Hurston was so poor when she died that her neighbors had to raise money for her funeral.
Marcel Proust wrote most of 'In Search of Lost Time' while bedridden in a cork-lined room to block out noise.
Calamity Jane claimed to have been married to Wild Bill Hickok, but historians have found no evidence of this.
Tennessee Williams was named Thomas Lanier Williams III and got his nickname from his college fraternity brothers.
Medieval Europeans believed bathing was unhealthy and could let disease enter through open pores, so wealthy people changed their linen undergarments instead of washing their bodies.
Ancient Romans used urine as mouthwash because the ammonia was thought to whiten teeth and kill bacteria.
In medieval times, people slept sitting up in beds because they believed lying flat was how corpses were positioned and might invite death.
Victorian women often died from their green dresses, which were dyed with arsenic-based pigments that poisoned them through skin contact.
Ancient Egyptians used moldy bread as an antibiotic treatment thousands of years before penicillin was officially discovered.
Medieval people ate off of thick slices of stale bread called 'trenchers' instead of plates, then gave the gravy-soaked bread to the poor or dogs.
In ancient Rome, purple clothing was so expensive that wearing it without permission from the emperor could result in death.
Medieval Europeans believed that taking a bath during menstruation would cause a woman to go insane.
Ancient Chinese emperors had food tasters not just for poison, but also to ensure the food was prepared exactly to temperature and seasoning preferences.
In medieval Europe, people thought tomatoes were poisonous because wealthy people died after eating them - the acid leached lead from their pewter plates.
Ancient Romans collected gladiator sweat and blood to sell as aphrodisiacs and healing ointments.
Medieval people brushed their teeth with twigs, salt, and sometimes powdered mouse heads for whitening.
In ancient Egypt, both men and women wore makeup daily, including green eyeshadow made from malachite that also repelled flies and prevented eye infections.
Medieval Europeans believed that a woman looking in a mirror during menstruation would tarnish the glass permanently.
Ancient Romans had heated floors in wealthy homes using a system called hypocaust, making them more comfortable than most homes until central heating was invented.
In medieval times, people thought that taking a bath during pregnancy would harm the baby, so pregnant women avoided washing for months.
Ancient Greeks and Romans used olive oil instead of soap, scraping it off with curved metal tools called strigils.
Medieval people believed that eating according to your astrological sign was essential for health - Scorpios needed spicy food, while Cancers required dairy.
In ancient China, long fingernails on men indicated wealth and status because it proved they didn't do manual labor.
Medieval Europeans thought that garlic could protect against vampires, but they actually hung it in homes to ward off the plague.
Ancient Romans paid taxes in urine because it was valuable for cleaning clothes and tanning leather.
In medieval times, people believed that washing your hair too often would cause madness, so most people washed it only a few times per year.
Ancient Mayans considered crossed eyes so beautiful that they would dangle objects in front of babies' faces to encourage the condition.
Medieval people thought that eating lettuce would cause sterility and nightmares, so it was often avoided by young couples.
In ancient Rome, left-handed people were considered unlucky and sinister - the Latin word 'sinister' literally means 'left'.
Medieval Europeans believed that drinking from a unicorn horn cup would neutralize any poison, so narwhal tusks sold for more than their weight in gold.
Ancient Egyptians shaved off their eyebrows when their cats died as a sign of mourning.
In medieval times, people thought that looking at ugly things during pregnancy would make their babies ugly, so pregnant women were surrounded by beautiful art.
Ancient Romans used lead pipes for plumbing and lead-based makeup, unknowingly poisoning themselves daily.
Medieval people believed that sleeping facing north would drive you insane, so beds were carefully positioned to avoid this direction.
In ancient Japan, blackened teeth were considered the height of beauty for married women, achieved by painting them with iron and vinegar.
Medieval Europeans thought that spices could prevent the plague, making pepper literally worth its weight in gold.
Ancient Vikings used to give new brides a kitten to ensure a happy marriage and good luck with future children.
In medieval times, people believed that eating beans would cause nightmares and disturb your soul, so monks were often forbidden from eating them.
Ancient Romans threw dinner parties that lasted for hours, with guests vomiting between courses to make room for more food.
Medieval people thought that drinking wine was safer than water - and they were often right, since alcohol killed many waterborne diseases.
In ancient Persia, important decisions were made twice - once while sober and once while drunk - and only implemented if both versions agreed.
Medieval Europeans believed that wearing emeralds would allow you to predict the future and reveal if your lover was faithful.
Ancient Aztecs valued cacao beans so highly that 100 beans could buy a turkey and counterfeiting them was punishable by death.
In medieval times, people thought that eating while angry would poison your food in your stomach, so meals began with prayers for peaceful hearts.
Ancient Romans believed that flamingo tongues were the ultimate delicacy and would serve them at the most extravagant banquets.
Medieval people used spider webs to stop bleeding wounds, and the practice actually worked because spider silk has natural antibiotic properties.
In ancient Egypt, workers building the pyramids were paid in garlic, onions, and radishes to keep them healthy and strong.
Medieval Europeans thought that wearing sapphires would protect them from envy and attract divine favor, making them popular with royalty.
Ancient Chinese foot binding was so valued that women with natural feet were often considered unmarriageable, regardless of their other qualities.
In medieval times, people believed that eating fish on Friday would guarantee their prayers were answered, leading to elaborate weekly fish markets.
Ancient Romans considered it polite to burp loudly during meals to show appreciation for the food and the host's hospitality.
Medieval people thought that wearing yellow indicated madness or betrayal, so it became the required color for prostitutes and heretics in many cities.
In ancient India, chess pieces were originally war elephants, chariots, cavalry, and infantry - reflecting real battle formations of the time.
Medieval Europeans believed that mandrake roots screamed when pulled from the ground and would kill anyone who heard them, so they used dogs to harvest them.
Ancient Mayans used chocolate as currency and believed cacao was more valuable than gold for trading everyday goods.
In medieval times, people thought that eating strawberries during pregnancy would give their babies red birthmarks shaped like strawberries.
Ancient Romans hired professional mourners called 'praeficae' to cry dramatically at funerals, with louder wailing indicating greater respect for the deceased.
Medieval people believed that keeping a piece of unicorn horn in your home would detect poison by changing color, making fake horns a thriving industry.
In ancient Greece, athletes competed naked in the Olympics because they believed clothing would slow them down and dishonor the gods.
Ancient Egyptians used sliced onions as currency and to pay workers building the pyramids.
Roman concrete gets stronger over time due to volcanic ash, while modern concrete deteriorates.
The ancient Greeks invented vending machines for holy water in temples over 2,000 years ago.
Mesopotamians created the first known recipe for beer, and it was considered safer to drink than water.
Ancient Chinese invented toilet paper in the 6th century, but it was only for emperors.
The Indus Valley civilization had better urban planning and sewage systems than most modern cities.
Maya astronomers calculated the length of a year more accurately than Europeans did until the 16th century.
Aztecs used cacao beans as currency and considered chocolate a gift from the gods.
Ancient Egyptians performed brain surgery and had a 60% success rate.
Romans brushed their teeth with urine because they believed it whitened them.
Greek fire was an ancient flamethrower weapon so effective its recipe was lost to history.
Ancient Babylonians created the first known customer complaint, carved in stone about poor copper quality.
Chinese emperors were buried with entire armies of terracotta soldiers to protect them in the afterlife.
Ancient Indians invented shampoo, plastic surgery, and calculated the value of pi to four decimal places.
Maya cities had populations larger than London during the same time period.
Aztec capital Tenochtitlan was built on a lake and had floating gardens that fed 200,000 people.
Ancient Egyptians kept baboons as pets and trained them to pick fruit from trees.
Ancient Greeks voted to exile politicians for 10 years if they became too powerful, called ostracism.
Mesopotamians invented the wheel, but used it for pottery for 300 years before transportation.
Ancient Chinese invented gunpowder but used it primarily for fireworks, not weapons.
The ancient Indian numeral system gave us the concept of zero and modern mathematics.
Maya developed a sophisticated writing system with over 800 hieroglyphic symbols.
Aztec children attended mandatory school regardless of social class, unusual for ancient civilizations.
Ancient Egyptians invented breath mints made from honey, myrrh, and cinnamon.
Romans had a goddess of sewers named Cloacina and built temples in her honor.
Babylonian mathematicians used a base-60 number system, which is why we have 60-second minutes.
Ancient Chinese invented paper money because carrying coins was too heavy for large transactions.
Indians invented chess, originally called Chaturanga, representing ancient army divisions.
Maya used chocolate in religious ceremonies and believed cacao trees were sacred.
Aztec warriors could achieve nobility through capturing enemies, regardless of birth status.
Ancient Egyptians invented eye makeup partly as sunscreen and insect repellent.
Romans built roads so well that many are still used today, 2,000 years later.
Ancient Greeks invented the alarm clock using water and pebbles.
Mesopotamians created the first known maps and the earliest system of laws.
The ancient Indian game of Snakes and Ladders taught moral lessons about karma.
Maya predicted solar eclipses centuries in advance with remarkable accuracy.
Aztecs practiced dentistry and used gold fillings for teeth.
Ancient Egyptians used crocodile dung as birth control.
Romans invented shopping malls with multiple levels and different types of stores.
Ancient Greeks used stones as toilet paper and voted with pottery shards.
Babylonians created the first known horoscopes and divided the sky into zodiac signs.
Indians developed the concept of infinity and advanced surgical techniques.
Maya created books from bark paper and had extensive libraries.
Aztecs required their rulers to be elected by a council, not inherit power.
Ancient Egyptians mummified millions of animals, including cats, birds, and crocodiles.
Romans had fast food restaurants called thermopolia with marble counters and serving holes.
Ancient Greeks invented the world's first computer, the Antikythera mechanism, to predict astronomical events.
Mesopotamians brewed over 20 different types of beer and had professional beer inspectors.
Ancient Chinese invented seismographs to detect earthquakes from hundreds of miles away.
Medieval Baghdad's House of Wisdom had a larger library than any European city would possess for centuries.
Mongol warriors could survive for days drinking only mare's milk and horse blood, making their armies incredibly mobile.
Tang Dynasty China had female emperor Wu Zetian, the only woman in Chinese history to rule in her own right.
Medieval Islamic hospitals had separate wards for different diseases, surgical suites, and even music therapy - centuries before European hospitals.
Viking women could request divorces and owned property independently, rights that many European women wouldn't gain for centuries.
The Mongol Empire at its peak was larger than the entire continent of Africa.
Medieval Chinese invented gunpowder, but initially used it for fireworks and scaring away evil spirits, not warfare.
Medieval Europeans believed unicorns were real animals that lived in India, and unicorn horns were sold for enormous sums.
The Tang Dynasty capital Chang'an had over one million residents, making it the world's largest city when London had barely 20,000.
Vikings used crystalline stones called 'sunstones' to navigate in cloudy weather, which actually worked by detecting polarized light.
Mongol leader Genghis Khan killed so many people that forests grew back over abandoned farmland, temporarily cooling the planet.
The medieval Chinese invented toilet paper in the 6th century, while Europeans were still using leaves and cloth.
Viking longships could sail in just three feet of water, allowing them to attack far inland via rivers.
The Abbasid Caliph Harun al-Rashid sent Charlemagne a living elephant as a diplomatic gift, which became a sensation in medieval Europe.
Medieval Chinese invented the compass, but initially used it for feng shui and fortune telling, not navigation.
The Mongols were religiously tolerant and exempt religious leaders of all faiths from taxation throughout their empire.
Medieval Islamic cities had street lighting, public hospitals, and sewage systems when European cities were dark and disease-ridden.
The Tang Dynasty had a postal system so efficient that messages could travel 1,200 miles in just six days.
Vikings discovered that eating polar bear liver would kill them, unknowingly avoiding vitamin A poisoning.
Medieval scholars in Cordoba, Spain translated and preserved ancient Greek texts that would have otherwise been lost forever.
The Mongol military used an early form of psychological warfare, spreading rumors of their brutality to make enemies surrender without fighting.
Medieval Chinese invented paper money during the Tang Dynasty, while Europeans were still using heavy metal coins.
Viking explorers reached as far south as North Africa and as far east as Constantinople, trading amber for silk and spices.
The medieval Islamic scholar Ibn Khaldun invented sociology and wrote about economic cycles 400 years before European economists.
Tang Dynasty China had ice cream made from milk, rice, and snow, served to the imperial court.
Medieval European knights typically fought on foot during battles, despite popular images of mounted cavalry charges.
Islamic Golden Age scholars invented the concept of the hospital as we know it, with medical schools, pharmacies, and patient records.
The medieval Chinese invented mechanical clocks with escapement mechanisms 300 years before they appeared in Europe.
The Mongol Empire had a primitive form of diplomatic immunity, protecting envoys and ambassadors throughout their vast territories.
Medieval Islamic mathematicians invented the decimal point and the concept of zero as a placeholder.
Tang Dynasty Chinese women played polo, a sport considered too dangerous for women in many later cultures.
Vikings practiced a form of democracy called the 'Thing,' where free men could vote on laws and settle disputes.
The medieval Islamic world had public libraries where anyone could borrow books for free, centuries before European public libraries.
Medieval Chinese invented playing cards, which eventually spread westward and evolved into modern card games.
Medieval European monasteries preserved literacy during the Dark Ages by copying manuscripts in scriptoriums.
Islamic Golden Age scholars invented distillation and created the first perfumes, medicines, and alcohols using this process.
The Tang Dynasty had a merit-based civil service system where anyone could take exams to become a government official.
Vikings buried their dead in boats because they believed it would help them sail to the afterlife.
The Mongol Empire facilitated the first truly global trade network, connecting Europe, Asia, and the Middle East.
Medieval Islamic scholars invented the astrolabe, an incredibly sophisticated astronomical computer used for navigation and timekeeping.
Chinese Tang Dynasty poets like Li Bai were considered rock stars of their time, with their verses memorized by people across social classes.
Vikings used ravens as navigation aids, releasing them at sea and following their flight toward the nearest land.
The medieval Islamic world had the first universities with degrees, academic freedom, and international student bodies.
Medieval Chinese invented woodblock printing 600 years before Gutenberg, mass-producing books and spreading literacy.
The Mongols adopted and improved the technologies of every culture they conquered, becoming master engineers and administrators.
Medieval European cathedrals took centuries to build, with some construction projects spanning 20 generations of workers.
Islamic Golden Age physicians performed complex surgeries including brain and eye operations with remarkable success rates.
The Tang Dynasty Chinese invented air conditioning using manually operated rotary fans and water-cooled air.

Potatoes were so feared in Europe that French peasants refused to eat them until Antoine-Augustin Parmentier posted armed guards around potato fields during the day, then removed them at night to encourage theft.
The Dutch once traded the island of Manhattan back to the English in exchange for a tiny spice island in Indonesia called Run.
Magellan's crew completed the first circumnavigation of the globe, but Magellan himself died halfway through the journey in the Philippines.
Spanish conquistadors introduced smallpox to the Americas, which killed an estimated 90% of the indigenous population before most Europeans even arrived.
The Portuguese were the first Europeans to reach Japan, arriving there before they reached Brazil.
Venice became fabulously wealthy by controlling European access to Asian spices, charging markups of over 1000% on black pepper.
The Aztec capital Tenochtitlan was larger and cleaner than any European city when Cortés arrived, with running water and daily waste removal.
Pirates were often employed by governments as privateers, making piracy a semi-official form of naval warfare during the colonial period.
The Dutch East India Company was the world's first multinational corporation and at its peak was worth more than Apple and Microsoft combined today.
Tobacco was so valuable in colonial Virginia that it was used as currency, and people paid taxes and fines with tobacco leaves.
The Spanish Empire was so vast that the sun literally never set on it, spanning from the Philippines to Peru to Florida.
Indigenous Americans taught European colonists how to grow corn, beans, and squash together in a technique called the 'Three Sisters,' which naturally fertilized the soil.
The city of Macau became the first and last European colony in China, lasting over 400 years under Portuguese rule.
European explorers were often searching for the mythical Christian kingdom of Prester John, believed to exist somewhere in Africa or Asia.
The Columbian Exchange introduced horses to the Americas and tomatoes to Europe, fundamentally changing both continents' ways of life.
